control_name: Solution component type is classified and correct public checklist path is selected
description: Determine whether the submission is Partner-Hosted, Customer-Deployed, Marketplace AMI/Container, Data/ML, etc., and select the corresponding public FTR checklist path.
why_it_matters: AWS publishes different FTR checklists by component type; validating the wrong one produces wrong findings and unsafe estimates.
required_evidence: A statement of deployment/hosting model (who operates the software and where it runs)
At least one architecture diagram or deployment description sufficient to classify component type
pass_signals: Submission explicitly states hosting model and matches the selected checklist type
Architecture matches the stated model (e.g., Customer-Deployed runs in customer account)
fail_signals: Hosting/deployment model cannot be determined from uploaded docs
Docs strongly suggest one model but self-assessment is for a different model
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if classification is probable but not explicit; fail=0 if cannot classify or is clearly mismatched.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Classification requires factual input from customer.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Add a one-page 'Solution Overview' stating hosting model, where components run, and who operates them; align the checklist type.
estimate_line_item_label: Classify solution type + select correct FTR checklist
reviewer_explanation_template: I could not reliably classify your solution type from the uploaded materials. Evidence needed: {{required_evidence}}. Without the correct checklist path, any pass/fail and any estimate is guesswork.
customer_summary_snippet: Your submission does not clearly state which FTR checklist applies, so the package is not safely validateable yet.
notes: Implement as a deterministic classifier with a 'cannot determine' outcome; do not guess.

control_name: Checklist/self-assessment version is within the public validity window
applies_to: All submissions that include an FTR checklist/spreadsheet or reference a checklist version.
description: Detect checklist version/date and confirm it is within the published validity/submission windows.
why_it_matters: AWS publishes checklist versions on a schedule and allows submission of previous versions only until specific dates; outdated versions create rework risk.
required_evidence: Checklist or self-assessment file name/version/date OR explicit statement of version used
pass_signals: Checklist version aligns to current published validity period or explicitly allowed previous window
fail_signals: Checklist is clearly older than the allowed submission window
Version cannot be determined
partial_credit_logic: pass=2; partial=1 if version not provable but appears current; fail=0 if clearly outdated.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Requires correct file selection.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Replace checklist/self-assessment with the current public version (or explicitly permitted previous version).
estimate_line_item_label: Update to current FTR checklist version
reviewer_explanation_template: Your checklist version appears to be {{observed_version}}. The public index shows versioning/submission windows; using an out-of-window checklist increases rejection/rework risk.
customer_summary_snippet: Use the current public checklist version (or an explicitly permitted previous version) to avoid rework.
notes: Parse dates from checklist HTML, filenames, and embedded metadata when possible.

control_name: Minimum artifact pack for the selected checklist path is present
description: Enforce that core artifacts required or repeatedly referenced by the public checklists are uploaded (at minimum: self-assessment + architecture diagram; plus deployment guide for Customer-Deployed).
why_it_matters: AWS Partner Central guidance says you upload self- assessment checklist and architecture diagrams plus other required/supplemental docs; Customer-Deployed requires deployment guide references for many items.
required_evidence: Self-assessment checklist/spreadsheet
Architecture diagram(s)
If Customer-Deployed: deployment guide (or equivalent) with page/section references
pass_signals: All minimum artifacts for the identified checklist path are uploaded
fail_signals: Missing self-assessment checklist/spreadsheet
Missing architecture diagram(s)
Customer-Deployed submission missing deployment guide
partial_credit_logic: pass=6; partial=3 if one non-core supporting doc is missing but core artifacts exist; fail=0 if any core artifact is missing.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Missing artifacts must be uploaded.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Upload the missing core artifacts (self-assessment, architecture diagram, and deployment guide if Customer-Deployed).
estimate_line_item_label: Assemble minimum FTR submission artifacts
reviewer_explanation_template: Core submission artifacts are missing: {{missing_list}}. AWS requires self-assessment + architecture diagrams, and Customer-Deployed controls require deployment-guide references. Without these, a pass/fail review is not honest.
customer_summary_snippet: Your package is missing required artifacts, so it cannot be validated for FTR readiness yet.
notes: Treat missing core artifacts as hard-stop. Do not quote payable remediation.

control_name: Self-assessment is complete (required fields answered; links/page refs provided when requested)
applies_to: All submissions containing a self-assessment spreadsheet/ checklist.
description: Check that all required self-assessment items are answered and that any required pointers (links/page/section/paragraph) are provided.
why_it_matters: Public checklists repeatedly request explicit page/ section/paragraph pointers for evidence; missing answers create rework and often block review.
required_evidence: Completed self-assessment spreadsheet/checklist with no blank required responses
Where checklist asks for link + page/section, those fields are filled
pass_signals: No blank required fields
Evidence pointers are present where required
fail_signals: Blank required fields
Evidence pointers missing on items that request them
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if <=10% of required fields are missing; fail=0 if >10% missing or key sections blank.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: normalize formatting of responses, standardize how page/section references are written, and insert placeholders for missing pointers. Not allowed: invent answers or evidence.
rewrite_template: For each checklist item with missing pointer, add: "{{missing_evidence_placeholder}}" and keep existing factual responses unchanged.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Fill missing checklist responses and add precise evidence pointers (link + page/section/paragraph).
estimate_line_item_label: Complete FTR self-assessment with evidence pointers
reviewer_explanation_template: Your self-assessment has missing or unreferenced items: {{missing_items}}. The checklist expects specific pointers (page/section/paragraph) so an AWS reviewer can verify quickly.
customer_summary_snippet: Several checklist items are incomplete or lack the required evidence pointers.
notes: Implement by parsing spreadsheet/CSV/structured fields; do not rely on LLM guesses.

control_name: Architecture diagram(s) clearly show AWS services/resources and how they connect
description: Ensure architecture diagrams detail how the solution interacts with AWS and name the AWS services used; for Customer-Deployed, diagrams in the guide must include all AWS services/resources in a typical deployment.
why_it_matters: Public checklists state architecture diagrams must detail AWS services/tools used and illustrate connections; missing clarity blocks validation.
required_evidence: Architecture diagram(s) that label AWS services and key data flows
For Customer-Deployed: diagram in deployment guide representing typical deployment
pass_signals: AWS services/resources are labeled (not just boxes)
Connections/flows between major components are depicted
fail_signals: Diagram is missing AWS service labels
Diagram is too abstract to infer deployed resources
partial_credit_logic: pass=5; partial=3 if services are present but some major flows unlabeled; fail=0 if diagram is essentially non-informative.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Diagrams must be corrected by the customer.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Create/update architecture diagrams: label AWS services, show connections, and include a typical deployment for Customer- Deployed.
estimate_line_item_label: Fix architecture diagrams for FTR clarity
reviewer_explanation_template: Your diagrams don’t clearly identify the AWS services/resources and how they connect. For FTR, diagrams must show how the solution uses AWS services in a typical deployment.
customer_summary_snippet: Your architecture diagrams are not yet detailed enough to validate the deployed AWS resources and connections.
notes: You can implement diagram parsing lightly (OCR/labels) but keep conservative: if ambiguous, mark as missing/partial.

control_name: No material contradictions across uploaded documents (model, data location, public exposure, support)
description: Detect contradictions that change risk posture or checklist applicability (e.g., hosting model, where sensitive data lives, whether public resources exist, support SLA claims).
why_it_matters: Contradictory submissions cannot be honestly validated and are unsafe to auto-remediate or estimate.
required_evidence: Consistent statements across docs about hosting model, data stores, encryption, public endpoints/resources, and support tiers
pass_signals: Narrative and diagrams align on component ownership and data flows
Security and support claims match the architecture model
fail_signals: Docs disagree on whether deployment is customer vs partner hosted
Docs claim 'no public resources' but also describe public buckets/ endpoints
Docs claim one support tier/SLA but another doc conflicts
partial_credit_logic: pass=6; partial=3 if only minor wording conflicts exist; fail=0 if any material contradiction exists.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Contradictions require factual resolution by the customer.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Resolve contradictions and update documents so model, data flows, and claims match 1:1.
estimate_line_item_label: Resolve contradictions across FTR artifacts
reviewer_explanation_template: Your documents contradict each other on: {{contradictions}}. Until those are resolved, producing a payable remediation quote would be irresponsible.
customer_summary_snippet: Your submission contains material contradictions that must be fixed before any reliable FTR readiness validation or estimate.
notes: Make contradictions a hard gate to consultation_only.

control_name: Unsupported or unverifiable claims are explicitly flagged (no fake evidence)
description: If a document asserts a control/outcome (e.g., 'CIS passed', 'encrypted', 'compliant', 'no public resources') without evidence in the uploaded pack, flag it as unsupported instead of guessing.
why_it_matters: AWS checklists require specific evidence; fabricating or overstating readiness destroys trust and can create partner program risk.
required_evidence: For each major security/reliability/compliance claim: a supporting section, config excerpt, or uploaded report (e.g., Security Hub export)
pass_signals: Major claims include citations/pointers to where substantiation exists in the pack
Claims are phrased conservatively when evidence is not included
fail_signals: Claims are definite but evidence is absent from the uploaded documents
Compliance claims appear with no internal validation process described
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if a small number of claims lack support; fail=0 if multiple high-stakes claims are unsupported.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: rewrite claims into evidence-linked language or add explicit placeholders. Not allowed: add new facts, results, certifications, or 'passed' statements.
rewrite_template: Replace unsupported definitive claims with: "Claim requires evidence: {{missing_evidence_placeholder}}" and keep the claim as a stated intent only if clearly labeled as intent.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Either upload evidence (reports/screenshots) or rewrite claims to be conditional and evidence-linked.
estimate_line_item_label: Fix unsupported claims (evidence-link or downgrade language)
reviewer_explanation_template: You state "{{claim}}" but I could not find supporting evidence in the uploaded pack. This is flagged as unsupported, not assumed true.
customer_summary_snippet: Some claims in the submission are not backed by evidence in the uploaded documents.
notes: Implement via claim-pattern detection + evidence lookup. Default to 'unsupported' not 'fail' unless contradicted.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: Hosting model runs entirely on AWS for critical components (HOST-001)
description: Validate that the docs state all critical application components run on AWS, with only the listed exceptions (edge CDNs, DNS, corporate IdP) and that third-party services processing application data are on AWS per checklist.
why_it_matters: The public Partner-Hosted checklist requires the product to run entirely on AWS (application and control planes) with limited exceptions.
required_evidence: Architecture diagram showing control plane and application plane locations
Narrative listing any third-party services that transmit/store/process application data
pass_signals: Docs explicitly state critical planes are on AWS and diagrams support this
Any exceptions match the checklist categories
fail_signals: Docs state critical components run on non-AWS infrastructure (not an allowed exception)
Third-party services process application data outside AWS without clear exception
partial_credit_logic: pass=6; partial=3 if mostly on AWS but one component location is unclear; fail=0 if critical components are clearly outside AWS.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. This is a factual architecture constraint.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Clarify hosting model and move critical components onto AWS (or re-scope offering so it matches the correct checklist type).
estimate_line_item_label: Align hosting model to Partner-Hosted FTR requirements
reviewer_explanation_template: Partner-Hosted FTR expects critical components to run on AWS. Your docs show: {{observed}}. This is not safely fixable via a blind documentation rewrite if the architecture is actually out of scope.
customer_summary_snippet: Your Partner-Hosted submission must run critical components on AWS; current evidence is missing or contradicts this.
notes: Treat clear non-AWS critical hosting as consultation_only.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: AWS Business Support (or Partner-led Support) or action plan exists (SUP-001)
applies_to: Partner-Hosted submissions; also referenced by Customer- Deployed prerequisites.
description: Verify docs show AWS Business Support (or higher / Partner- led Support) for production accounts OR a written action plan for issues requiring AWS Support.
why_it_matters: Public checklists require Business Support tier (or action plan) for production accounts.
required_evidence: Support plan statement (Business Support / Partner-led Support) OR an action plan for AWS Support escalation
List of what is considered 'production accounts' for the offering
pass_signals: Explicit statement of support coverage or a concrete action plan
Escalation path and ownership are named
fail_signals: No mention of AWS support coverage and no action plan
partial_credit_logic: pass=3; partial=1 if support coverage stated but no actionable escalation plan; fail=0 if absent.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: structure the support plan text and add missing fields as placeholders. Not allowed: claim subscription level without evidence.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Document AWS support tier or create an action plan for issues requiring AWS Support, with escalation owners and steps.
estimate_line_item_label: Document AWS support coverage + escalation plan
reviewer_explanation_template: Support coverage is required (Business Support or an action plan). I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: Your package needs a clear AWS support/ escalation plan for production accounts.
notes: Do not accept vague statements like 'we can contact AWS if needed' without steps/owners.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: Periodic architecture review program exists and references AWS shared responsibility (WAFR-001/WAFR-002)
description: Verify docs state periodic architecture reviews (at least annually) using a documented standard (Well-Architected is recommended) and that shared responsibility for security/resiliency is acknowledged and addressed.
why_it_matters: Public Partner-Hosted checklist requires annual reviews and explicitly references AWS shared responsibility models.
required_evidence: Statement of review cadence (>= yearly) and standard used
Document section mapping operational responsibilities between partner and customers
pass_signals: Annual review cadence is stated
A standard is named (e.g., Well-Architected) and shared responsibility is addressed
fail_signals: No review cadence/process is documented
Shared responsibility is ignored
partial_credit_logic: pass=3; partial=1 if cadence exists but shared responsibility not addressed (or vice versa); fail=0 if neither exists.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: reorganize existing text into a standard template and add placeholders. Not allowed: claim a review happened if no evidence.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Add an architecture review policy (annual cadence) and include an explicit shared-responsibility section.
estimate_line_item_label: Establish architecture review + shared responsibility documentation
reviewer_explanation_template: The Partner-Hosted checklist expects at least annual architecture reviews and explicit shared responsibility coverage. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: Add an annual architecture review cadence and document partner vs customer responsibilities for security and resiliency.
notes: Accept internal standards if documented; do not require Well- Architected explicitly.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: Incident management plan exists and is maintained (ARC-005)
description: Verify an incident management plan exists with roles/steps and evidence it is maintained current.
why_it_matters: Public checklist requires developing incident management plans.
required_evidence: Incident management plan document (roles, triage steps, communications, post-incident process)
Last updated date or change log
pass_signals: Plan exists with clear roles and steps
Plan includes a maintenance signal (updated date, revision history)
fail_signals: No incident plan in the uploaded pack
Plan is a one-liner with no operational detail
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if plan exists but lacks maintenance signal; fail=0 if absent.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: restructure the plan for clarity and add placeholders for missing sections. Not allowed: invent incident procedures or contacts.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Create or expand an incident management plan and add revision history/ ownership.
estimate_line_item_label: Write/update incident management plan
reviewer_explanation_template: Incident management is required. I found {{evidence}}. Missing or too thin: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: You need an actionable incident management plan (roles, steps, and maintenance), not just a statement that you handle incidents.
notes: Treat absence as high impact; if combined with other gaps, consider consultation_only.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: CIS/Security Hub evidence is provided for automated-account-scan controls
applies_to: Partner-Hosted submissions, especially when claiming strong security posture.
description: Check whether a CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark report / Security Hub export (or equivalent) is uploaded to substantiate CIS-linked controls.
why_it_matters: AWS public guidance describes automated validation using Security Hub (or equivalent). Without a report, document-only claims about account configuration are not reliable.
required_evidence: Security Hub export (CSV) or CIS benchmark report, or screenshots showing benchmark status for in-scope accounts
pass_signals: A Security Hub/CIS report is uploaded and clearly in-scope
fail_signals: No automated-scan evidence is included but docs make strong claims like 'CIS compliant' or 'all controls passed'
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if screenshots exist but not exportable/complete; fail=0 if absent and strong claims exist.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Evidence must be uploaded.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Export Security Hub/CIS results (or provide screenshots) for in-scope accounts and include in the submission pack.
estimate_line_item_label: Generate and attach CIS/Security Hub evidence
reviewer_explanation_template: Partner-Hosted validation often relies on automated account checks. I did not find a CIS/Security Hub report in your upload, so I cannot credibly validate CIS-linked configuration controls.
customer_summary_snippet: Upload CIS/Security Hub evidence if you want configuration-related controls validated from documents.
notes: Do not require this for every case, but treat it as necessary for high-confidence security posture claims.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: Credentials are not embedded in code or documentation (IAM-009)
applies_to: Partner-Hosted submissions; also relevant to Customer- Deployed docs if they instruct customers.
description: Detect any instructions or examples that embed long-term access keys, passwords, or secrets directly in code/config files or documentation.
why_it_matters: Public Partner-Hosted checklist explicitly requires not embedding credentials in application code; AWS IAM best practices discourage long-term keys and poor secret handling.
required_evidence: Documentation examples sanitized of real secrets
Stated approach for providing credentials at runtime without embedding
pass_signals: No embedded credentials in docs/examples
Docs instruct using roles/secret stores rather than hardcoding
fail_signals: Docs instruct customers/operators to paste access keys into config files
Actual secrets appear in uploaded materials
partial_credit_logic: pass=6; partial=3 if examples exist but are clearly placeholders and emphasize not committing secrets; fail=0 if any real secret or instruction to hardcode long-term creds exists.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: sanitize examples (replace with placeholders) and add warnings. Not allowed: change the underlying auth approach or claim a secure mechanism exists if not described.
rewrite_template: Replace any credential-like strings with placeholders (e.g., "<AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID>") and add: "Do not hardcode credentials. Use IAM roles or a secret store."
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Remove credential hardcoding from docs and adopt a role/secret-store based approach; scrub any leaked secrets immediately.
estimate_line_item_label: Remove embedded credentials from docs/code examples
reviewer_explanation_template: I found embedded credentials or instructions to hardcode them: {{evidence}}. This is a hard-stop security issue; it cannot be fixed safely by wording tweaks alone.
customer_summary_snippet: Your docs must not embed or encourage hardcoding credentials; this is a hard-stop security issue.
notes: Treat discovery of real secrets as incident: advise rotation; do not store them.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: Secrets are stored securely with encryption and access controls (IAM-010)
applies_to: Partner-Hosted submissions; relevant when product uses secrets (DB creds, tokens, keys).
description: Check that docs describe secure centralized secret storage (e.g., Secrets Manager or Parameter Store SecureString) with encryption and access controls, and auditability.
why_it_matters: Public checklist recommends purpose-built secret management and requires encryption/access control/auditability properties.
required_evidence: Named secret store approach (service/feature) and how secrets are accessed
Statement that secrets are encrypted at rest and transmitted over TLS
pass_signals: Docs explicitly describe centralized secret storage and access controls
Encryption-at-rest and TLS-in-transit are stated
fail_signals: Secrets are stored in plaintext files or ad-hoc stores without access controls
No description of secret handling exists
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if a secure store is named but access control/audit details are missing; fail=0 if secrets are plaintext or undocumented.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: reorganize existing secret-handling description and add placeholders for missing details. Not allowed: claim a secret store exists if not described.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Document and implement secure secret storage (Secrets Manager or Parameter Store SecureString) with least-privilege access and audit trail.
estimate_line_item_label: Document/implement secure secrets management
reviewer_explanation_template: Secrets must be stored securely (encrypted, access-controlled, auditable). I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: Your submission should clearly explain how secrets are stored and accessed securely (not ad-hoc plaintext).
notes: If secrets approach implies product changes, keep estimate conservative or move to consultation_only.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: No unrestricted SSH/RDP exposure in security groups (NETSEC-001)
applies_to: Partner-Hosted submissions with VPC/EC2 usage or managed compute where SGs apply.
description: Validate evidence shows no security group allows ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22 or 3389; and default security groups restrict all traffic (as referenced by the Partner-Hosted checklist).
why_it_matters: Public checklist mandates least-permissive security group rules and explicitly calls out open SSH/RDP as unacceptable; Security Hub also treats these as high severity controls.
required_evidence: Security Hub or CIS benchmark results showing relevant controls pass OR screenshots/config excerpts proving restrictions
pass_signals: Uploaded report shows no unrestricted 22/3389 ingress
Docs explicitly state admin access is restricted and evidence is provided
fail_signals: Report shows failing controls for unrestricted 22/3389
Docs explicitly instruct opening 22/3389 to the world
partial_credit_logic: pass=6; partial=3 if evidence is partial (screenshots) but indicates restriction; fail=0 if report shows fail or docs encourage open access.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. This is a security posture issue.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Close unrestricted SSH/RDP exposure; restrict admin access paths and provide evidence (Security Hub export or equivalent).
estimate_line_item_label: Fix unrestricted SSH/RDP exposure evidence
reviewer_explanation_template: The Partner-Hosted checklist expects least-permissive SG rules (no 0.0.0.0/0 to 22/3389). Evidence found: {{evidence}}. This is a hard-stop if failing.
customer_summary_snippet: Unrestricted SSH/RDP exposure is a hard-stop. Provide Security Hub/CIS evidence showing it’s not present.
notes: Prefer structured reports; screenshots are partial credit only.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: Automatic backups exist and restores are periodically tested (BAR-001/BAR-002)
applies_to: Partner-Hosted submissions that store any stateful data.
description: Check docs describe automated backups for stateful stores and include evidence of periodic recovery/restore tests aligned to RTO/RPO.
why_it_matters: Public checklist requires automatic backups and periodic recovery testing to verify backup integrity and objectives.
required_evidence: Backup policy/procedure describing what is backed up and how often
At least one restore/recovery test record (date + outcome) OR explicit procedure and schedule
pass_signals: Automated backup mechanism is documented and covers stateful stores
Restore/recovery testing is documented with at least one completed test
fail_signals: No backup approach is documented for stateful data
Backups exist but no restore test evidence and no schedule
partial_credit_logic: pass=6; partial=3 if backups are documented but restore testing evidence is missing; fail=0 if backups are absent or explicitly not done.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: improve structure and add placeholders for missing test evidence. Not allowed: invent test results or claim restores were tested.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Document automated backups and run at least one restore test; capture evidence (date, steps, outcome).
estimate_line_item_label: Backup/restore documentation + test evidence
reviewer_explanation_template: Backups and restore testing are required. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}. If you can’t restore, you can’t claim reliability.
customer_summary_snippet: Backups must be automated and restores must be tested; missing either is a hard readiness gap.
notes: If stateful stores are unclear, mark missing and prompt for inventory.

control_name: Partner-Hosted: RTO/RPO are defined and resilience testing is performed (RES-001/RES-002/RES-004)
description: Verify RTO and RPO are explicitly stated and that resilience testing includes at least AZ impairments, performed at least annually and prior to approval per checklist.
why_it_matters: Public checklist requires RTO/RPO and a resilience test cadence, including a completed test prior to approval.
required_evidence: RTO value
RPO value
Resilience test evidence (date + scope + results) or documented test plan and last completion
pass_signals: RTO and RPO are stated with units
At least one resilience test is documented with date and scope
fail_signals: RTO/RPO missing
No resilience test evidence or plan
partial_credit_logic: pass=5; partial=3 if RTO/RPO are defined but resilience test evidence is missing; partial=2 if resilience test exists but only one of RTO/RPO exists; fail=0 if none exist.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: reformat RTO/RPO and test-plan sections; add placeholders for missing test evidence. Not allowed: invent test execution results.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Define RTO/RPO and complete a resilience test (at minimum AZ impairment). Document results.
estimate_line_item_label: Define RTO/RPO + run resilience test
reviewer_explanation_template: RTO/RPO and resilience testing are expected. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}. Without tested resilience, reliability claims are not credible.
customer_summary_snippet: Define RTO/RPO and provide evidence of resilience testing; otherwise reliability posture is unproven.
notes: Treat 'we are highly available' without RTO/RPO and tests as unsupported.

control_name: Partner-Hosted (conditional): Cross-account access uses roles + external ID/issuer URL + least privilege (CAA-001/CAA-002/CAA-007 etc.)
applies_to: Only if the solution accesses customer AWS accounts (assume- role, web identity, or similar).
description: Validate docs specify cross-account role-based access (not customer-provided static creds), use external ID or dedicated issuer URL to prevent confused deputy, and provide least-privilege role/policy setup guidance or automation (e.g., CloudFormation).
why_it_matters: Public Partner-Hosted checklist specifies cross-account role usage and external ID/issuer URL requirements to prevent confused deputy risks.
required_evidence: Role-based access description (AssumeRole/AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity)
External ID or issuer URL approach (partner-generated, unique, not customer editable)
Provisioning guidance or automation for least-privilege policies
pass_signals: Customer access is via cross-account roles
External ID/issuer URL is described and is partner-generated + unique + view-only
Least-privilege setup guidance exists
fail_signals: Docs request customers provide static IAM user access keys for integration
No external ID/issuer URL mentioned for role assumption
Policies appear overly broad with no least-privilege guidance
partial_credit_logic: N/A=3; pass=3; partial=1 if roles exist but external ID/issuer URL details are missing; fail=0 if static customer-provided credentials are required or external ID is customer-controlled.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. This is an integration security design requirement.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Redesign cross-account access to use roles + external ID/issuer URL + least privilege; update docs and automation accordingly.
estimate_line_item_label: Fix cross-account access design (roles + external ID + least privilege)
reviewer_explanation_template: Your solution integrates with customer AWS accounts. The checklist expects roles plus external ID/issuer URL protections (confused deputy). Evidence found: {{evidence}}. Gaps: {{gaps}}.
customer_summary_snippet: If you access customer AWS accounts, you must use cross-account roles with external ID/issuer URL protections and least- privilege setup guidance.
notes: Only apply if documents indicate cross-account integration.

control_name: Customer-Deployed: Introductory material includes use cases (INT-001)
description: Verify the deployment guide (or intro material) describes concrete use cases for the solution and the submission points to where it is documented.
why_it_matters: Customer-Deployed checklist explicitly requires use cases in introductory material with evidence pointers.
required_evidence: Deployment guide section describing use cases
Pointer: page number + section title + paragraph (or equivalent stable reference)
pass_signals: Use cases are described clearly
Pointers are provided
fail_signals: No use cases described
No pointers provided
partial_credit_logic: pass=2; partial=1 if use case exists but pointers are missing; fail=0 if missing.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: improve clarity and structure of use-case description, without adding new use cases or claims.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Add a clear use-cases section to the deployment guide and reference it precisely in the checklist.
estimate_line_item_label: Add use-cases section to deployment guide
reviewer_explanation_template: Customer-Deployed packages must include use cases in the deployment guide. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: Your deployment guide needs a clear use-cases section with precise evidence pointers.
notes: This is doc-only and safe to estimate.

control_name: Customer-Deployed: Intro includes typical deployment overview and resource list (INT-002)
description: Verify intro includes an overview of a typical customer deployment and lists the resources set up after deployment, with evidence pointers.
why_it_matters: Customer-Deployed checklist explicitly requires a typical deployment overview and list of resources created.
required_evidence: Deployment guide overview section
List/table of AWS resources created (by service/type)
Pointer: page/section/paragraph
pass_signals: Deployment overview exists and is concrete
Resources created are listed
fail_signals: No resource list
Only vague text ('deploys infrastructure')
partial_credit_logic: pass=3; partial=1 if overview exists but resource list is incomplete; fail=0 if both missing.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: restructure into a clear list/table and normalize AWS service naming, using only already-mentioned resources.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Add a typical deployment overview and a concrete resource inventory to the deployment guide.
estimate_line_item_label: Add deployment overview + resource inventory
reviewer_explanation_template: The checklist expects a typical deployment overview and a list of resources created. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: Document what gets deployed (and what resources are created), in concrete AWS terms.
notes: If the architecture diagram conflicts with the resource list, also fail contradiction rule.

control_name: Customer-Deployed: Architecture diagrams include AWS services/resources, connections, VPC/subnets, integrations; use AWS icons (ARCH-001/ARCH-004/ARCH-005/ARCH-006)
description: Validate the deployment guide includes diagrams showing all deployed AWS services/resources and how they connect, plus network diagrams showing VPCs/subnets and integration points; diagrams use AWS Architecture Icons.
why_it_matters: Customer-Deployed checklist explicitly requires these diagram properties and references AWS Architecture Icons.
required_evidence: Diagram(s) in deployment guide
Evidence pointers to the diagram pages
Use of AWS Architecture Icons (or explicit statement with link)
pass_signals: Diagrams show AWS resources and connections
VPC/subnets present when networking is non-trivial
Integrations shown
AWS icons used
fail_signals: Diagrams omit major AWS services
No VPC/subnet depiction where relevant
No integration points shown despite being referenced
Non-AWS icons and no mapping/legend
partial_credit_logic: pass=4; partial=2 if diagrams exist but fail one sub-requirement (e.g., icons); fail=0 if diagrams are missing or too incomplete.
rewrite_policy: No rewrite. Diagrams must be regenerated by customer.
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Update deployment guide diagrams to meet checklist requirements and use official AWS icons.
estimate_line_item_label: Fix Customer-Deployed architecture diagrams
reviewer_explanation_template: Customer-Deployed diagrams must show AWS resources/connections, VPC/subnets, and integrations, and use AWS icons. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: Your deployment guide diagrams must be complete and use AWS icons; right now they are missing required elements.
notes: Treat missing diagrams as hard-stop via required_artifacts rule.

control_name: Customer-Deployed: Guide warns against root use and provides least-privilege guidance (DSEC-002/DSEC-003)
description: Verify deployment guide explicitly warns customers not to use root for deployment/operations and provides prescriptive least-privilege guidance for access granted during deployment.
why_it_matters: Customer-Deployed checklist requires these security guidance items; AWS root-user best practices recommend avoiding root for daily tasks.
required_evidence: Deployment guide section warning against root usage
Deployment guide section on least privilege for roles/policies created
pass_signals: Explicit warning against root usage
Concrete least-privilege guidance (scoped actions/resources/conditions)
fail_signals: Docs instruct using root for deployment
No least-privilege guidance
partial_credit_logic: pass=5; partial=2 if root warning exists but least- privilege guidance is weak (or vice versa); fail=0 if docs require root or omit both.
rewrite_policy: Allowed: strengthen wording and structure of the warning and least-privilege guidance using existing facts. Not allowed: claim the product does not require root if docs indicate it does.
rewrite_template:
## Security: Do Not Use Root
Do not use the AWS account root user for deployment or operations.
## Security: Least Privilege
Grant only the permissions required for deployment and operation. {{existing_details_or_placeholder}}
missing_evidence_placeholder: MISSING EVIDENCE: {{missing_item}}. This was not found in the uploaded files. Add it (or upload a document) before claiming FTR readiness.
remediation_summary: Add explicit root-user warning and prescriptive least-privilege guidance to the deployment guide.
estimate_line_item_label: Add root/least-privilege security guidance
reviewer_explanation_template: Customer-Deployed guidance must warn against root usage and explain least privilege. I found: {{evidence}}. Missing: {{missing_items}}.
customer_summary_snippet: If your deployment requires root or lacks least-privilege guidance, it’s not safely FTR-ready.
notes: If docs require root privileges, treat as consultation_only (likely product change).
